content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: Tom is ___ (tall) boy in his class — he is almost 1.9 metres.
distractor_rationale: 'most' is used with long adjectives only; 'tall' is a one-syllable adjective and takes '-est'
superlatives require 'the' before them; omitting 'the' is a common learner error
'more tall' is a comparative form, not a superlative, and 'the more tall' mixes structures incorrectly

content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: This is ___ (hot) day of the whole summer — everyone is staying inside.
distractor_rationale: 'most' is incorrect with one-syllable adjectives like 'hot'; the correct form doubles the final consonant and adds '-est'
the spelling is wrong — 'hot' requires doubling the final consonant before '-est', giving 'hottest' not 'hotest'
superlatives require 'the' before them; omitting 'the' is a frequent learner error

content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: That old sofa is ___ (cheap) item in the whole furniture shop.
distractor_rationale: 'cheap' is a one-syllable adjective and takes '-est', not 'most'
superlatives require 'the' before them; omitting 'the' is a common learner error
using both 'most' and '-est' together is a double superlative, which is always incorrect in standard English

content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: Anna said the science exam was ___ (difficult) test she had ever taken at school.
distractor_rationale: 'difficult' is a three-syllable adjective and cannot take '-est'; it must use 'most'
using both 'most' and '-est' together is a double superlative and is always grammatically wrong
superlatives require 'the' before them; omitting 'the' is a common learner error

content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: This restaurant serves ___ (good) pizza in the whole city, according to every food review I have read.
distractor_rationale: 'good' is an irregular adjective; its superlative is 'best', not 'most good'
'goodest' is a non-standard invented form; the irregular superlative of 'good' is 'best'
'better' is the comparative form of 'good', not the superlative; 'best' is required here

content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: Of all the athletes in the competition, Maria ran ___ (fast) and finished the race in record time.
distractor_rationale: 'fast' is a one-syllable adverb and takes '-est' to form the superlative, not 'most'
using both 'most' and '-est' creates a double superlative, which is always incorrect
'faster' is the comparative adverb form used when comparing two items, not a group; the superlative is needed here

content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: The new apartment block near the park is one of ___ (expensive) buildings in the neighbourhood.
distractor_rationale: 'expensive' is a three-syllable adjective and cannot take '-est'; it must use 'most'
the 'one of the + superlative + plural noun' structure requires 'the' before the superlative
'more expensive' is a comparative form, not a superlative; 'most expensive' is required after 'one of the'

content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: Although several candidates applied for the position, David performed ___ (badly) at the interview and was not offered the job.
distractor_rationale: 'badly' is an irregular adverb; its superlative is 'worst', not 'most badly'
'baddest' is a non-standard invented form; the irregular superlative adverb of 'badly' is 'worst'
'the least well' means the opposite direction of comparison — the lowest degree of performing well — whereas 'the worst' directly expresses the poorest performance, which is what the context demands

content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: The project manager chose the design that had been completed ___ (efficiently) of all the teams, since deadlines were critical.
distractor_rationale: 'efficiently' is a multi-syllable adverb and cannot take '-est'; it must use 'most'
superlative adverbs with 'most' require 'the' before them; omitting 'the' is a common learner error
'more efficiently' is a comparative adverb form used to compare two items; the superlative 'most efficiently' is required when comparing all teams

content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: Surprisingly, the ___ (little) popular dish on the menu — a plain vegetable soup — turned out to be the one that sold out first.
distractor_rationale: 'most little' is not a valid superlative form; 'little' has an irregular superlative 'least' when used as an adverb of degree modifying 'popular'
'less popular' is the comparative form used to compare two items, not the superlative needed to describe the lowest rank within the whole menu
'fewest' is the superlative of 'few' and refers to countable nouns, not to a degree of popularity modifying an adjective

content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: Tom is ___ (tall) student in the whole class.
distractor_rationale: most is used only with long adjectives; tall is a one-syllable adjective and takes -est
omitting the before a superlative is grammatically incorrect in English
more is used for comparatives, not superlatives; the superlative of tall is tallest

content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: This is ___ (bad) film I have ever seen at the cinema.
distractor_rationale: bad is an irregular adjective; its superlative is worst, not most bad
baddest is a non-standard invented form; the correct irregular superlative is worst
double superlative combining most and worst is grammatically impossible

content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: Out of all the dishes on the menu, the vegetable soup was ___ (cheap) option we could order.
distractor_rationale: cheap is a one-syllable adjective and forms its superlative with -est, not with most
the definite article the is required before a superlative adjective in this structure
least cheapest is a double superlative and contradicts the meaning; the correct form is the cheapest

content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: Anna speaks ___ (fluently) of all the candidates who interviewed for the position.
distractor_rationale: fluently is an adverb and requires most to form its superlative; the alone cannot create a superlative
the definite article the is required before the superlative adverb most fluently
placing most after the adverb is incorrect word order; the correct structure is the most + adverb

content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: The coastal path near the village is one of ___ (scenic) walking routes in the entire region, attracting visitors throughout the year.
distractor_rationale: scenic is a two-syllable adjective ending in a consonant cluster and does not take -est; it requires most
combining most with -est creates an impossible double superlative form
the definite article the is obligatory in the structure one of the most + adjective + plural noun

content.instruction: Choose the correct option to complete the sentence.
content.prompt: This is ___ bag in the shop — it costs only two euros.
distractor_rationale: most cheap is incorrect because 'cheap' is a one-syllable adjective that forms its superlative with -est, not 'most'
omitting 'the' before a superlative adjective is a common learner error; 'the' is required
more cheap is a comparative form, not a superlative, and also incorrectly uses 'more' with a short adjective

content.instruction: Choose the correct option to complete the sentence.
content.prompt: July is ___ month of the year in our city — temperatures reach 40 degrees.
distractor_rationale: most hot is incorrect because 'hot' is a one-syllable adjective that takes -est, not 'most'
omitting 'the' before the superlative is a grammatical error; 'the' is obligatory
hotest is a spelling error — the final consonant must be doubled before -est for short CVC adjectives

content.instruction: Choose the correct option to complete the sentence.
content.prompt: Anna runs ___ of all the students in her class — she always finishes first.
distractor_rationale: most fast is incorrect because 'fast' is a one-syllable adverb that forms its superlative with -est
the most fastest is a double superlative, which is always incorrect in standard English
faster is a comparative adverb form used to compare two people, not a superlative comparing within a whole group

content.instruction: Choose the correct option to complete the sentence.
content.prompt: That was ___ film I have ever seen — I fell asleep twice during it.
distractor_rationale: boringest is incorrect because 'boring' is a two-syllable adjective ending in -ing, which requires 'most' rather than -est
most bored is incorrect because 'bored' describes a person's feeling, not a film's quality; the sentence describes the film itself
omitting 'the' before the superlative is a grammatical error; 'the' is obligatory with superlatives

content.instruction: Choose the correct option to complete the sentence.
content.prompt: Mark scored ___ on the maths test — he only got three answers correct out of twenty.
distractor_rationale: most bad is incorrect because 'bad' has an irregular superlative form 'worst'; 'most bad' does not exist in standard English
baddest is incorrect because 'bad' is irregular and does not take -est; the correct form is 'worst'

the least good is grammatically possible in some contexts but is semantically wrong here — it implies he was barely good, whereas the context shows he performed very poorly, making 'the worst' the only natural choice

content.instruction: Choose the correct option to complete the sentence.
content.prompt: The blue dress is ___ of all the ones she tried on, so she decided not to buy it.
distractor_rationale: the most unattractive, while structurally possible, changes the meaning by using a negative adjective rather than the 'the least' construction that the sentence structure calls for; the sentence tests the 'the least + adjective' superlative pattern specifically
attractivest is incorrect because 'attractive' is a multi-syllable adjective that requires 'most' or 'least', never -est
less attractive is a comparative form used to compare two items, not a superlative comparing within a group of all the dresses she tried

content.instruction: Choose the correct option to complete the sentence.
content.prompt: The Pacific Ocean is ___ ocean on Earth — it covers more than 165 million square kilometres.
distractor_rationale: most large is incorrect because 'large' is a one-syllable adjective that takes -est to form its superlative
the most largest is a double superlative, combining both 'most' and -est, which is always wrong in standard English
omitting 'the' before the superlative adjective is a grammatical error; 'the' is required when using a superlative before a noun

content.instruction: Choose the correct option to complete the sentence.
content.prompt: The new software update runs ___ of all the versions they have released, even on older computers.
distractor_rationale: the most efficient is an adjective superlative and cannot modify the verb 'runs'; an adverb superlative is required here
efficientliest is not a real word; multi-syllable adverbs always use 'most' or 'least' to form the superlative
omitting 'the' before a superlative adverb is a grammatical error; 'the' is required in superlative adverb constructions

content.instruction: Choose the correct option to complete the sentence.
content.prompt: The old library on the corner is ___ buildings in the whole district, having been built over two hundred years ago.
distractor_rationale: one of the most old is incorrect because 'old' is a one-syllable adjective that forms its superlative with -est, not 'most'
the oldest is grammatically correct as a superlative but does not fit the 'one of the + superlative + plural noun' structure required by the plural noun 'buildings' later in the sentence
one of the older uses a comparative form, which cannot be used in the 'one of the + superlative + plural noun' construction

content.instruction: Choose the correct option to complete the sentence.
content.prompt: After testing all five candidates, the manager agreed that Sara had performed ___ during the interview process and offered her the job immediately.
distractor_rationale: goodest is an incorrect regularisation of the irregular adverb 'well'; the superlative of 'well' is 'best', not 'goodest'
the most good is incorrect because 'good' used as an adverb takes the irregular superlative 'best', not 'most good'
the most well is incorrect because 'well' has an irregular superlative form 'best'; 'most well' does not exist in standard English

content.instruction: Find and correct the grammar mistake in the sentence.
content.prompt: This is most tall building in our city.
content.error_sentence: This is most tall building in our city.
content.corrected_sentence: This is the tallest building in our city.

content.instruction: Find and correct the grammar mistake in the sentence.
content.prompt: My dog is the goodest pet I have ever had.
content.error_sentence: My dog is the goodest pet I have ever had.
content.corrected_sentence: My dog is the best pet I have ever had.

content.instruction: Find and correct the grammar mistake in the sentence.
content.prompt: January is coldest month of the year in this region.
content.error_sentence: January is coldest month of the year in this region.
content.corrected_sentence: January is the coldest month of the year in this region.

content.instruction: Find and correct the grammar mistake in the sentence.
content.prompt: That was the most bad meal I have ever eaten at a restaurant.
content.error_sentence: That was the most bad meal I have ever eaten at a restaurant.
content.corrected_sentence: That was the worst meal I have ever eaten at a restaurant.

content.instruction: Find and correct the grammar mistake in the sentence.
content.prompt: Sara works the most hard of everyone in the office, so she deserves a bonus.
content.error_sentence: Sara works the most hard of everyone in the office, so she deserves a bonus.
content.corrected_sentence: Sara works the hardest of everyone in the office, so she deserves a bonus.

content.instruction: Find and correct the grammar mistake in the sentence.
content.prompt: The science exam was the most longest test we had taken all term, and many students ran out of time.
content.error_sentence: The science exam was the most longest test we had taken all term, and many students ran out of time.
content.corrected_sentence: The science exam was the longest test we had taken all term, and many students ran out of time.

content.instruction: Find and correct the grammar mistake in the sentence.
content.prompt: This hiking trail is one of the most popular trail in the national park, attracting thousands of visitors each summer.
content.error_sentence: This hiking trail is one of the most popular trail in the national park, attracting thousands of visitors each summer.
content.corrected_sentence: This hiking trail is one of the most popular trails in the national park, attracting thousands of visitors each summer.

content.instruction: Find and correct the grammar mistake in the sentence.
content.prompt: Although the new model is expensive, it is clearly the least cheapest option when you consider how long it will last.
content.error_sentence: Although the new model is expensive, it is clearly the least cheapest option when you consider how long it will last.
content.corrected_sentence: Although the new model is expensive, it is clearly the least expensive option when you consider how long it will last.
